230707.explanation: VZ wants residents to be able to vote additionally on important municipal decisions. First through an advisory referendum. This allows the municipality to better listen to your wishes. If the constitution changes, we also want a binding referendum so that you can undo wrong decisions.

230709.explanation: VZ wants to increase property tax (OZB) only if your total housing costs do not rise. First, waste costs must be reduced by new tenders. Only when waste is affordable again will a higher OZB be negotiable.

230711.explanation: Together with other parties, VZ has already significantly extended store opening hours recently, especially on Sundays. Most residents are satisfied with this. If there is enough support for even more extended opening hours, VZ is open to that. A new Shopping Hours Act may come from The Hague.

230712.explanation: VZ does not want to spend money on 'Sun on Land' projects in our municipality. We want to keep the municipality affordable and avoid unnecessary costs. Initiatives from local energy cooperatives are welcome, but VZ believes they must finance their plans themselves.

230713.explanation: VZ opposes the Bed-Bad-Brood scheme. This reception for asylum seekers who must leave the Netherlands sends the wrong signal and costs the municipality money without providing a solution. Rejected asylum seekers must leave within 28 days and are responsible for their return.

230714.explanation: VZ does not want priority for status holders in social housing. We want to house status holders assigned to our municipality by The Hague in temporary flexible housing during their stay. This keeps the social rental homes of Wold and Waard available for our own residents.

230715.explanation: VZ wants a service-oriented municipality with lower waste costs. Therefore, we believe that dropping off bulky waste, such as a broken sofa or an old bed, should be free. This keeps the municipality affordable and helps residents in a practical way.

230716.explanation: VZ wants to protect the agricultural character of Westerkwartier. Therefore, we oppose solar parks on agricultural and nature land and against tall wind turbines. The landscape, livability, and farmers are our top priority. VZ opposes building another two large solar parks in Westerkwartier.

230717.explanation: VZ welcomes additional cultural events that are also appreciated by a broad audience. Beautiful events strengthen community bonds and keep our traditions alive. We want an active municipality that embraces such initiatives and actively thinks along with you.

230718.explanation: VZ wants to invest in modern school buildings so that children receive good education in their own environment. Many schools are outdated, so renewal is needed, but not everywhere and all at once. We will only do this if it is financially responsible and keeps the municipality affordable for you.

230719.explanation: VZ wants real safety, not just rules on paper. Nuisance and drug problems are increasing, so firm enforcement is needed. If additional BOA officers help tackle this, we support increasing their number. This keeps our villages safe and livable.

230720.explanation: VZ does not want a coffeeshop in our municipality. It causes nuisance and sends the wrong signal, especially now that drug use among youths is increasing. The council has already rejected this earlier. Those seeking a coffeeshop can go to the city of Groningen.

230721.explanation: VZ wants cameras in nuisance hotspots to increase safety. They help tackle youth nuisance, drug problems, and speed maniacs who cause very serious accidents. Cameras support targeted enforcement and make our villages visibly safer and more livable.

230722.explanation: VZ wants BOA officers to be able to work safely. A taser can help stop dangerous situations quickly and without heavy violence. It prevents physical fights, reduces injury risks, and provides an appropriate tool for de-escalation. Use only after good training and with strict rules.

230723.explanation: VZ wants mixed residential neighborhoods with different types of housing. New plans must include 30% social housing and two-thirds of the homes must be affordable. This creates neighborhoods where people from different backgrounds meet. This promotes community spirit and livability.

230724.explanation: VZ wants to introduce a mandatory owner-occupancy rule as soon as we learn that investors are buying up too many homes. This can counter speculation and help keep houses affordable. Because this buying problem does not yet exist in our municipality, VZ considers this measure too far for now.

230725.explanation: VZ does not want interference from the Welstand committee (Libau) in small construction projects. Further, we want officials to think along with you in a solution-oriented way. This gives our 41 communities space to realize initiatives and strengthen livability.

230726.explanation: VZ wants to shift poverty reduction efforts somewhat from families with children to working poor and single pensioners (AOW recipients), who often still lack support because they are unknown to the municipality. With targeted help, lower waste costs, and early support for debts, we help everyone!

230727.explanation: Westerkwartier has chosen for years for minimum provision schemes instead of exemption. This targeted approach combats poverty more effectively. There is much support, especially for poorer families with children. In the 'Potjeswijzer' you can find what support is available in the municipality.

230728.explanation: Due to housing shortages, elderly and young people stay at home longer, causing the number of cars at existing homes to increase. Especially old neighborhoods and senior housing have too few parking spaces. To prevent neighborhood conflicts, VZ wants more parking spaces especially here.

230729.explanation: VZ does not want unnecessary rules. Where street safety is at risk, we want appropriate measures. In many built-up areas, introducing a 30 km/h speed limit is unnecessary.

230730.explanation: Thanks in part to the VZ alderman, more distribution points for free menstrual products have already been realized recently. We have not heard residents say that more are needed.

230731.explanation: VZ does not want separate citizen councils or a youth council. In practice, mainly higher-educated people participate, while practically educated people show little interest. As a result, no good representation of our residents arises. That is undesirable for VZ.

230732.explanation: VZ does not want separate citizen councils or a youth council. In practice, mainly higher-educated people participate, while practically educated people show little interest. As a result, no good representation of our residents arises. That is undesirable for VZ.

230733.explanation: VZ wants officials to engage more often with residents so they better see what is happening and thus improve service delivery. Preferably at people's homes; another good option is extra counters, for example in a library, community center, or at Novatec, so support remains nearby.

230734.explanation: VZ cherishes associations and volunteers. They are essential for a livable society. The municipality must abolish unnecessary rules and permits and provide practical support when requested.

230735.explanation: VZ wants fair rates and rules for all sports facilities. A level playing field is the starting point. Making exceptions for a single association does not help achieve this.

230736.explanation: VZ values the many sports and cultural associations and their volunteers. Because they largely manage themselves and the municipality already contributes sufficiently, VZ considers extra cultural funding irresponsible in these financially difficult years.

230737.explanation: VZ is proud of the commitment of the people of Aduard to preserving their library. We are not aware of any demand for a swimming pool: residents have not expressed this wish to VZ, and the VZ village alderman has not heard this wish from the village either.

230738.explanation: VZ wants a strong local economy. SMEs, including farmers, increasingly get stuck in piles of rules that add little to real improvements. Lower costs speed up permits, stimulate local entrepreneurship, and keep our villages lively. VZ chooses trust and simplicity.

230740.explanation: A lower price for burial rights sounds appealing but heavily strains the municipal budget. A reduction also means less money for management, maintenance, and accessibility of cemeteries. VZ chooses quality, dignity, and financial responsibility; therefore, no reduction.

230741.explanation: VZ sees opportunities to attract more businesses thanks to the strategic location and growth of Westerkwartier. More business parks provide start-ups with affordable space, shared facilities, and a network to grow faster. This strengthens innovation and employment in our villages.

230742.explanation: VZ wants shelter facilities in the region itself. We already accommodate many Ukrainian refugees. If The Hague still mandates extra asylum accommodation, it should only be in small, safe locations near service villages. This is not in the middle of residential neighborhoods. VZ always advocates careful placement after consultation with you.

230744.explanation: Electric cars are still unaffordable for many residents. Therefore, VZ does not want unregulated growth of charging stations that take up parking space. First, assess actual demand and intelligent distribution, only then expand. This way, our villages remain livable.

230745.explanation: VZ does not want paternalism. Hospitality businesses in Westerkwartier currently have no fixed closing time, and this works well. Only in cases of serious nuisance can the mayor intervene. A mandatory 12:00 AM closing time does not fit a free, lively, and livable Westerkwartier.

230749.explanation: VZ believes residents should be able to decide about trees on their own property. A felling permit leads to unnecessary rules and delays. We trust residents to responsibly manage their own land. VZ stands for less bureaucracy, more personal control.

230750.explanation: The municipality must build over 2,400 homes by 2030. Housing plans show that only near Leek and Zuidhorn can a new neighborhood of 400 houses fit. In Leek, the best location is near Tolbertervaart North. VZ finds this necessary to truly build enough affordable homes.

230753.explanation: VZ currently sees a vacancy tax as unnecessary. In small villages, vacancies mainly result from low demand, not unwillingness. Extra charges won't help. We choose trust, fewer rules, and customized solutions. For Leek's center, a targeted plan against retail vacancies will be introduced.

230754.explanation: VZ is against its own credit bank. Westerkwartier already works efficiently with the GKB from Assen. A separate bank costs a lot, requires extra staff, and doesn't provide better assistance. Cooperation is cheaper, faster, and keeps support for residents reliable.

230755.explanation: Volunteers are indispensable for livability in Westerkwartier. VZ wants volunteers to be able to apply for a VOG certificate free of charge so that costs do not pose a barrier. This enhances safety, supports associations, and appreciates everyone’s volunteer efforts in our villages.

230756.explanation: VZ wants new and safe bike paths, but not at the expense of space for cars. In the vast Westerkwartier, cars remain necessary for many residents. Good bike paths and sufficient road space for cars together ensure accessibility, safety, and livability in all villages.

230759.explanation: VZ opposes completely free public transport for low-income individuals because it costs a lot of money that could otherwise go to care, livable villages, or safety. Moreover, free public transport doesn't help everyone: many residents live outside bus routes. VZ chooses targeted support, not expensive measures with too little effect.

230760.explanation: Youth care costs rose from 17 to 30 million in a few years. VZ believes youth assistance must be cheaper and more efficient! Too much money goes to unnecessary help while young people with serious problems are not well assisted. Better cooperation and less bureaucracy can reduce costs.

230761.explanation: Free couples therapy for low-income individuals sounds social, but according to VZ, it is not a good idea. It costs a lot, reaches only a small group, and falls outside the municipality’s core tasks. VZ chooses targeted support focusing on problems that are municipal responsibilities.

230762.explanation: VZ opposes expanding the smoking ban to more public places. Outdoor smoking causes little nuisance, and extra bans lead to unnecessary paternalism. VZ favors clear rules where necessary, but no further restriction of freedom in public spaces.

230763.explanation: VZ believes that homeless people should not automatically receive rental housing. First shelter, guidance, and addressing problems. Only then transition to housing. This way, we truly help people and keep housing allocation fair for all residents.

230764.explanation: VZ supports informal caregivers with respite care, practical help, and good support. But giving a monetary amount is not a good idea: it costs a lot, is difficult to arrange fairly, and turns caregiving into a financial transaction. Help should relieve, not reward.

230707.explanation: Residents should be able to participate early. A referendum can help with major decisions about the living environment.

230709.explanation: Tax pressure must remain as low as possible. Before raising property taxes (OZB), we first critically review expenditures; if an increase is necessary, it should be for broad core tasks, not just sports/culture.

230711.explanation: Entrepreneurs must be able to conduct business with fewer unnecessary rules. Expanded Sunday opening hours can be allowed, with room for local consideration and entrepreneurs’ choice.

230712.explanation: We support local energy initiatives but not structural municipal subsidies for generation. First save energy (insulation) and put solar panels on roofs. The municipality can facilitate with permits/advice, provided it is small-scale and fits the landscape.

230713.explanation: The municipality must act humanely and safely and follow the law. Emergency shelter may be necessary to prevent nuisance and insecurity; however, structural asylum accommodation and exceptions are primarily a national government task.

230714.explanation: In case of scarce social rental homes, the municipality should prioritize its own residents and vulnerable households. Priority based on status does not fit here, within the legal framework.

230715.explanation: Waste separation and reuse must be accessible. Free bulky waste disposal prevents dumping and encourages recycling, as long as it remains affordable.

230716.explanation: The landscape is leading: no solar parks on land. We focus on solar panels on roofs and first saving energy with insulation.

230717.explanation: Cultural activities strengthen neighborliness and livability. We want fewer restrictive rules around village activities and more room for initiatives.

230718.explanation: Good, healthy, and sustainable school buildings are important for equal opportunities. We invest and lobby the national government for sufficient funding.

230719.explanation: Safety for our residents is paramount. Enforcement is necessary when rules are violated. We ensure sufficient BOA capacity and resources, emphasizing visibility and prevention.

230720.explanation: A coffeeshop can cause extra nuisance and safety problems. We choose prevention, tackling nuisance, and a safe living environment.

230721.explanation: No general camera surveillance, but targeted and temporary cameras at places with serious nuisance are allowed. This must not come at the expense of street patrols.

230722.explanation: We want to properly equip BOAs, but tasers increase the risk of escalation. Prefer training, de-escalation, bodycams, and cooperation with the police.

230723.explanation: Affordable housing for everyone is essential. A robust social component in new construction helps starters and lower-income people, with attention to a good mix in each village.

230724.explanation: Scarce owner-occupied homes should remain available to residents. A self-occupancy obligation prevents speculation and helps starters and families in the municipality. We assume this concerns homes built with subsidies.

230725.explanation: Reducing regulatory pressure can be done with zones free or low in aesthetic regulations, and there are sufficient other means and options to preserve the village and cultural-historical character.

230726.explanation: We want more people with low income to get faster and better help, with a human approach and one point of contact. First ensure everyone who currently is entitled to schemes uses them. No automatic income limit increases; only targeted if demonstrably necessary.

230727.explanation: Municipal charges should be waived as much as possible for residents with low income. This fits with a human approach and prevents debts.

230728.explanation: More parking spaces are not always the solution and often come at the expense of greenery and livability. We look per neighborhood together with residents at the best design.

230729.explanation: Traffic measures must suit the situation. We choose targeted measures where the neighborhood requests them, not the same 30 km/h limit everywhere.

230730.explanation: No one should be without basic products due to lack of money. Additional distribution points can help, especially for young people and low-income individuals.

230731.explanation: We focus on low-threshold participation (polls, neighborhood apps) and a referendum where necessary. A citizens' assembly is not the most efficient form for our municipality, partly because it risks being dominated by one-sided groups.

230732.explanation: Young people must remain involved in policies that affect them. A youth council can assist with this and strengthen local democracy.

230733.explanation: Services should be close by. In larger centers, we want decentralized counters so residents are quickly helped with a single point of contact.

230734.explanation: Rules around village activities are often too restrictive. Small events should be allowed without a permit, with a notification requirement and clear safety agreements where necessary.

230735.explanation: Associations and communities have strong organizing capacity. The municipality supports where needed, but taking over sports fields is appropriate only in exceptional situations.

230736.explanation: Culture and associations strengthen cohesion and livability in our communities. However, a lot of money is already spent to maintain these facilities.

230737.explanation: A new swimming pool is a major investment and brings ongoing costs. We opt to preserve existing pools and improve facilities that benefit most residents.

230738.explanation: We want fewer municipal additional environmental regulations and less regulatory layering for SMEs. Legal environmental standards remain, but the municipality simplifies, speeds up, and makes procedures predictable while helping find solutions.

230740.explanation: We want to keep tax pressure low, but rates must be cost-covering and fair. Reductions are possible only if financially responsible, and current prices are often already below actual cost.

230741.explanation: More space for starting entrepreneurs fits an entrepreneur-friendly municipality. Shared workspaces can stimulate innovation and employment, preferably in existing buildings.

230742.explanation: If this obligation is imposed by the national government, it applies that: location choices require customization and support. Shelters should not be placed by default in residential neighborhoods; we involve residents early and choose a location that fits safety and livability.

230744.explanation: We want to make sustainability progress, but no arbitrary targets. Charging points must be rolled out based on demand, be affordable, and fit with public space and grid capacity.

230745.explanation: Hospitality is important for vibrant centers and the local economy. Closing times should be tailored, with enforcement against nuisance where needed.

230749.explanation: We strongly support simplifying and reducing rules where possible. We believe our residents are very capable of managing this themselves. We trust there is sufficient awareness for the necessity and preservation of nature.

230750.explanation: Housing construction must fit the villages and landscape. We build affordable and appropriate homes, distributed across communities, so the character does not fundamentally change.

230753.explanation: We prefer to tackle vacancy by encouraging reuse and simplifying rules. An additional tax is only a last resort and can also affect local owners.

230754.explanation: We prefer to organize debt assistance and loans through existing, professional partners. A municipal credit bank is complex, risky, and may incur extra costs.

230755.explanation: Volunteers are indispensable for community spirit and associations. A free Certificate of Conduct lowers barriers and encourages participation in sports, culture, and care.

230756.explanation: Westerkwartier is a rural municipality with service villages and residential villages in a large area that makes car transport necessary. Safe bicycle routes are important. We seek the best balance per location between car, bike, and safety.

230759.explanation: Free public transport for low-income individuals sounds nice but is expensive. We prefer to focus on better public transport connections and targeted support without increasing tax pressure.

230760.explanation: Youth care is a priority. We invest in prevention and learn from best practices; cuts often make problems bigger and more expensive.

230761.explanation: Support is important, but couples therapy mainly belongs in care and insurance. The municipality can focus on prevention and low-threshold help.

230762.explanation: Prevention and health are important. A smoking ban where children play or around schools can contribute to a healthier environment, with clear enforcement.

230763.explanation: Homelessness requires quick help, but not automatic allocation of a (permanent) rental home. We offer shelter or temporary housing with guidance where needed and work on throughput through the regular housing allocation system, with customization.

230764.explanation: Informal caregivers deserve appreciation and support, but an annual fixed cash amount is not always the best or affordable form. Prefer respite care, practical help, and tailored solutions.

230707.explanation: Referenda reflect current public opinion. Elected representatives must look further and make decisions for everyone, including long term. They take minorities, vulnerable people, and future generations into account.

230709.explanation: The Christian Union wants to be cautious with raising property tax but does not exclude increases above indexation. We prefer to use property tax increases to maintain important facilities, as we have done with the increase that made investments in sports and culture possible.

230711.explanation: Our deepest wish is to continue celebrating Sunday as a collective day of rest. Employees should not feel pressured to work on Sundays. We also want church services to be organized in peace and churchgoers to attend unobstructed.

230712.explanation: It is indeed important that residents receive support, but especially practical support.

230713.explanation: Fair national distribution: every municipality shares responsibility for asylum seeker reception. We cooperate with regional municipalities and inform residents in time about changes (location, size, target group). The Christian Union believes every person deserves dignity, also rejected asylum seekers.

230714.explanation: The Christian Union believes municipalities must take their legal duty to house status holders seriously and sees housing as part of humane reception and integration.

230715.explanation: Not completely free because solid cost coverage is also needed. But we also want to better inform and support residents in waste separation.

230716.explanation: A deal is a deal - the Christian Union stands for reliable governance.

230717.explanation: The Christian Union finds it important that villages in Westerkwartier are livable, with room for creativity and appreciation for art and culture. A rich cultural offering with music, dance, theater, and design contributes to meaning, connection, and attractiveness.

230718.explanation: School buildings that are planned and need it will receive our financial support in the coming four years.

230719.explanation: Shared responsibility for safety: safety starts with community, with people who know, help, and protect each other.

230720.explanation: We are against coffee shops and against municipalities growing weed. Drugs do not belong in our society.

230721.explanation: Cameras seriously infringe on people's privacy. Therefore, we are cautious about deploying cameras. In case of nuisance in neighborhoods or villages, we opt for a targeted approach together with residents, police, and municipality. This way, we find sustainable solutions together.

230722.explanation: BBO officers are not police officers; they supervise and are not meant for strict enforcement. Therefore, no tasers for BBO officers, but rather more neighborhood police and solutions through dialogue with citizens and neighborhood watch.

230723.explanation: The Christian Union supports 30% social housing in new construction in Westerkwartier. This ensures affordable homes for starters, families, and the elderly. This way, we build a livable municipality for everyone.

230724.explanation: These measures concern preventing the quick resale of new-build homes for profit, in order to avoid speculation.

230725.explanation: Not necessarily everything, but it should not be unnecessarily complicated.

230726.explanation: We believe the current standards regarding minimum policy should be maintained. We support people not only financially but also strengthen their independence and future prospects. The Christian Union wants to effectively combat poverty and debt with fair and accessible policies. Our goal: work must always pay off and no one should live in poverty.

230727.explanation: The Christian Union wants to consolidate separate funds and schemes into supplementary benefits, so residents can make their own choices. Additionally, we want a social collection and debt remission policy for municipal taxes and levies so that residents with financial worries do not get further into trouble. So, if necessary, the municipality will step in!

230728.explanation: We especially want more greenery in the neighborhoods. Regarding parking, we opt for smart solutions such as carpool spots, shared cars, and more bicycle use. This way, we keep our neighborhoods green, livable, and affordable for everyone.

230729.explanation: 30 km/h as the default speed limit within built-up areas, except for public transport and connecting roads where 50 km/h remains allowed.

230730.explanation: It is important that every resident who needs it has free and easy access to menstrual products.

230731.explanation: Citizen assemblies add an additional decision-making layer, which makes things more complex and less transparent for our residents. The Christian Union prefers low-threshold participation with all residents through village discussions and tailored approaches so everyone can participate.

230732.explanation: The Christian Union actively involves young people, but not through a separate council. We seek dialogue via political cafés or a youth platform to share ideas.

230733.explanation: The Christian Union opposes more municipal service desks in every village: the municipality focuses on digital services and mobile consultation hours to keep costs low. This allows customized services per village without expensive physical desks.

230735.explanation: Many sports fields are already municipally owned; some have been privatized. We are open to returning them to municipal management if associations request it.

230736.explanation: Whether it costs more is uncertain, but the Christian Union wants children, associations, and residents to have ample opportunities for creative development because this strengthens both individuals and society. If more funds are needed for this, we are ready to have that discussion.

230737.explanation: The municipality has a clear overview of the need for facilities and makes a (financial) annual plan based on that. We strive for a fair and balanced distribution of sports facilities throughout the municipality.

230738.explanation: Less regulatory burden and more customization where possible. Entrepreneurs must be able to apply for permits quickly and easily. The municipality thinks constructively and seeks workable solutions, even where national rules are restrictive.

230740.explanation: We want to keep burial fees at the current level and believe they do not have to be cost-covering.

230741.explanation: Christian Union Westerkwartier supports more startup buildings. This attracts young entrepreneurs, stimulates employment, and strengthens the local economy. This way, we make our municipality attractive for the future.

230742.explanation: Small-scale housing, preferably in the neighborhood, to see and get to know each other. Small-scale shelters, appropriately sized for the population centers in our municipality. The government arranges housing and expenses; organizations such as churches help connect people with society.

230744.explanation: More charging points for electric cars (at least 1,000–1,500 by 2030), matching the growth of electric vehicles. Smart charging stations that contribute to a stable electricity grid and are placed in logical locations.

230745.explanation: The current regulations suit Westerkwartier. We have received no signals that these should be changed, so we see no reason to do so.

230749.explanation: The Christian Union wants an annual review of which rules can be simplified or abolished.

230750.explanation: Our wish and ambition are to realize 500 new homes annually, spread across the villages, according to the housing plans established in recent years. Large-scale housing projects are necessary to reach this annual target.

230753.explanation: We will engage in dialogue with the owners of those properties to find a joint solution.

230754.explanation: The Christian Union is against a municipal credit bank. We favor active cooperation with housing corporations, energy companies, and health insurers to identify payment arrears early. Sufficient expertise exists within the municipality to assist residents directly without unnecessary referrals.

230755.explanation: Volunteers can apply for a free Certificate of Conduct (VOG) if needed.

230756.explanation: Roads heavily used by students will be made safer where necessary, for example with separate bicycle paths or extra lighting. Special attention is given to the Mensumaweg. This route will be designed so that cyclists, especially students, can travel safely and confidently. Safe cycling routes take priority so young people can use them to safely get to school. Mixed traffic fits better with a livable municipality than asphalt designed solely for cars.

230759.explanation: The Christian Union is not automatically for free public transport. Residents should have a voice in this, depending on personal need. Residents have self-direction: the municipality determines eligibility, residents decide on spending.

230760.explanation: The municipality must keep youth care accessible for vulnerable children despite shrinking budgets and longer waiting lists. This requires investing in families and communities, where the government promotes shared care and guarantees that professional help remains available for those who need it.

230761.explanation: Free relationship therapy for people who cannot afford it (up to 130% of the statutory social minimum), as an extension of the divorce desk of De Schans.

230762.explanation: Working towards a smoke-free generation: protecting growing children from tobacco smoke and vapes; more smoke-free environments (e.g., squares).

230763.explanation: We work according to Housing First to end homelessness for good: everyone gets a (temporary) home with the right support to maintain it, in a neighborhood where people accept each other. We use the Ethos Light definition to also recognize less visible homelessness.

230764.explanation: Volunteers and informal caregivers are indispensable. Their work deserves appreciation and support, not extra barriers. We want less bureaucracy, more encouragement, and better support so they experience more trust and less control.

230707.explanation: We are in favor of a corrective referendum. As preparation, GroenLinks-PvdA wants to organize at least one citizen assembly during the next council term, as a trial, on an important and complex subject.

230709.explanation: Reasoned and substantiated, this could also apply to other topics. GroenLinks-PvdA wants to increase the property tax (ozb) by more than inflation correction if truly necessary. That requires political courage. We have already shown this with the motion from July 2025.

230711.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to continue allowing supermarkets and other stores to be open at some time on Sundays.

230712.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA supports stimulating sustainable energy and cooperatives of residents in this. We promote and support energy cooperatives, energy collectives, and other shared energy provisions.

230713.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to contribute to reception upon request and supports implementation of the Distribution Act (which spreads asylum seekers over all municipalities).

230714.explanation: Good reception fits in GroenLinks-PvdA’s philosophy: refugees are among the various urgent groups.

230715.explanation: Our starting point is: waste is a raw material. We want as much reuse as possible, and for what truly remains, the polluter pays. Moreover, waste collection should naturally be as cheap as possible, but cost-covering.

230716.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA is not in favor of large-scale interventions by solar parks. But those two large parks are agreed-upon policy because they are necessary for the transition to sustainable energy. Research is currently being conducted. The outcome may conclude, for example, that smaller fields will be developed.

230717.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to expand the availability of cultural and social activities in the villages.

230718.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to quickly catch up on the backlog in the renewal and sustainability of school buildings. A solid plan requires priority in the budget plus additional resources, for example from Nij Begun.

230719.explanation: The number of BOAs (special enforcement officers) has already been expanded recently. There are currently no indications that more are needed. If it really turns out to be necessary, we can expand further. GroenLinks-PvdA wants to primarily invest in proximity in the coming period: enough permanent neighborhood police officers and youth workers in villages who are visible and accessible.

230720.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA supports legalizing soft drugs and is therefore not fundamentally against a coffee shop, but at this moment there are no known applications.

230721.explanation: If there is really a lot of nuisance, cameras can help. GroenLinks-PvdA wants fair and accessible safety: no ethnic profiling, no repression without cause, but clear action where there is really a lot of nuisance.

230722.explanation: BOAs should have sufficient (self-protection) resources to act.

230723.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants two-thirds of the housing in the plans to be affordable: with at least thirty percent social rental housing and housing for elderly and youth, forty percent affordable ownership/rental, and thirty percent expensive ownership/rental.

230724.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants homes to be used for living, not as profit objects. However, tightening the mentioned three years can also cause significant problems for residents.

230725.explanation: Abolishing the aesthetic regulations leads to a deterioration of quality and more neighbor/neighborhood disputes. a landscape quality map is the basis for good policy and management.

230726.explanation: The municipality must investigate how many 'working poor' there are in the municipality and develop policy to support that group or groups and provide assistance to people at or up to 140% of the minimum income (currently 130%).

230727.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants necessary support with payment problems and poverty. This may include the waiver of levies.

230728.explanation: There are parking standards the municipality must comply with. Providing more parking spaces for cars above that affects safety and livability.

230729.explanation: This fits with GroenLinks-PvdA's pursuit of greater safety and a clean environment.

230730.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to combat period poverty. We provide menstrual products free of charge as essential necessities at fixed points.

230731.explanation: In the coming council period, we will organize at least one citizens' assembly as a pilot on an important and complex topic.

230732.explanation: We want children and youth to be more involved in decision-making. This can be done, for example, by forming a youth council in the municipality or by actively involving children and youth in thinking about topics and/or specific policies in other ways.

230733.explanation: There are already quite a few points that function like a municipal desk: in a village hall, library, and places where municipal buildings are located.

230734.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA supports a simple application process, but we also want attention paid to the environment, pollution, and risks. At the same time, we want things to be simpler through proper support: we are generous with permits for parties and festivals.

230735.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to keep sports fields and buildings in municipal ownership. Privatization is unwise for various reasons. It poses great risks for sports clubs as volunteer organizations. Moreover, their long-term survival then becomes less certain.

230736.explanation: If really necessary, the property tax (ozb) can increase by more than the inflation correction. This requires political courage. We have already shown that with the motion from July 2025 for the benefit of sports and cultural facilities.

230737.explanation: The honest truth is that it will already be difficult enough to keep the existing pools open. But if there is sufficient support and capacity in Aduard, then we can certainly try.

230738.explanation: Everyone, including SMEs, contributes to a better environment. Everyone must comply with the rules.

230740.explanation: We want reasonable rates for grave rights. Support options are available for those with insufficient incomes.

230741.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants to support networks of and for self-employed persons (zzp’ers) and help realize shared workspaces and/or shared offices.

230742.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants refugee housing to be dispersed, in small locations, and in villages.

230744.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA aims for a future with bicycles and cars powered electrically or even with hydrogen. That means there must be sufficient charging points.

230745.explanation: We do not want to impose restrictions, provided the rules to prevent nuisance are met.

230749.explanation: These trees can also be characteristic features or important for biodiversity in the area.

230750.explanation: If such a suitable (new) location fits well into the landscape and does not affect unique areas (such as Middag Humsterland and the coulisse landscape), then it is a good idea to reduce the housing shortage.

230753.explanation: GroenLinks wants an active policy against vacancy and leaving building land unused without demonstrable force majeure. This can include warnings and fines after six months.

230754.explanation: See the results of research on this: the costs for the municipality are much higher than now and the risks disproportionately high. In addition, there is insufficient specialist knowledge possible.

230755.explanation: Being able to apply for a Certificate of Conduct (VOG) free of charge fits with supporting volunteer work.

230756.explanation: A catch-up is needed for bicycle paths. That is why they are given some priority.

230759.explanation: We want to organize public transport for those who cannot afford it themselves; we are considering allowing people below a certain income threshold to use public transport free of charge.

230760.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes the national government should provide sufficient funds for this decentralized task.

230761.explanation: If this fits the indication, it is possible.

230762.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA supports such a prevention policy and a smoke-free generation.

230763.explanation: Such an offer fits with working for urgent target groups.

230764.explanation: We support informal caregivers with other measures. We will ensure further expansion of respite care (temporary replacement), practical assistance, and opportunities for informal caregivers to have a say themselves.

230707.explanation: A referendum can be a good tool, but careful consideration is needed regarding how and on which issues it should be used.

230709.explanation: Although sports and culture desperately need investments, there may also be other reasons for an increase that residents benefit from significantly.

230711.explanation: D66 wants to enable extended opening hours without making a distinction between supermarkets and other stores.

230712.explanation: The municipality can look into a kind of revolving fund for these joint initiatives.

230713.explanation: In the city of Groningen, there is a 'Bed, Bath, Bread' facility for the entire region, so there are no plans for one in Westerkwartier.

230714.explanation: People who have just received a residence permit should be housed quickly, as well as others who are urgently seeking a new roof over their heads for valid reasons.

230715.explanation: Disposal of waste must be priced fairly and cost-effectively; free services do not fit. If people truly cannot afford it, arrangements are available.

230716.explanation: Agreements have been made with the region and national government that only two large solar parks may be built in Westerkwartier. As a reliable government, you do not simply undo agreements.

230717.explanation: If there is demand and capacity (police, nuisance) to organize more cultural events, then D66 naturally supports that.

230718.explanation: D66 supports healthy schools but is also realistic about the municipality's financial possibilities.

230719.explanation: The number of enforcement officers (boa's) has been significantly increased recently. Without very good reasons, we will not hire more boa's.

230720.explanation: Currently, coffee shops often operate with money whose origin and destination are 'unclear.' This undermines society. As long as this is the case, we are opposed.

230721.explanation: Camera surveillance is very expensive, requires many rules, and is not very effective. It's better to invest money in prevention.

230722.explanation: There is no indication that enforcement officers currently need a weapon such as a taser. That is the police's responsibility.

230723.explanation: This is standing policy, with which D66 fully agrees.

230724.explanation: D66 sees this as a way to curb speculation on the housing market, but there must be room for customization based on personal circumstances.

230725.explanation: Although more experimentation with looser rules is possible, residents do expect new buildings to fit into the surroundings.

230726.explanation: Partly due to D66's efforts, Westerkwartier has become more attractive for people receiving social assistance to start working, especially for those with children. Debt counseling still deserves more attention.

230727.explanation: Levies apply to everyone equally. People who cannot afford them can be accommodated, up to and including exemption.

230728.explanation: There is little space to add more parking in existing neighborhoods, and much more parking space makes new housing even less affordable.

230729.explanation: This is standing policy that D66 Westerkwartier supports.

230730.explanation: The policy to make menstrual products more widely available was partially initiated by D66.

230731.explanation: D66 considers citizens' assemblies useful for important issues.

230732.explanation: Good idea, combine youth input with a citizens' assembly. D66 finds it important that young people are involved in the future of Westerkwartier.

230733.explanation: Previously, D66 was in favor, but current practice shows that a single counter combined with local agreements works well enough and saves a lot of money.

230734.explanation: Small events currently do not require a permit; only a notification is needed. D66 Westerkwartier wants to maintain this.

230735.explanation: The municipality must consult well with associations that no longer want to own their field for whatever reason. In most cases, the municipality already owns it.

230736.explanation: D66 is positive about active participation of Westerkwartier residents in cultural activities (music lessons, choir, photography, courses), whether or not through associations.

230737.explanation: D66 expects there is no need for a swimming pool in Aduard, as there are already pools in Electra, Zuidhorn, and Groningen. A renovated ice rink is preferred.

230738.explanation: SMEs must also comply with environmental regulations. However, the municipality can support SME owners with knowledge if there is a need.

230740.explanation: Grave rights fees are intended for the management and maintenance of cemeteries. This is already far from cost-effective; investing more public money in this is not appropriate now.

230741.explanation: D66 sees this as a great opportunity for new businesses to make a successful start.

230742.explanation: Integration works best right in the middle of society.

230745.explanation: Current agreements for hospitality and events are functioning well.

230749.explanation: Trees are of great social importance and deserve protection. A permit requirement for trees with a trunk circumference over 35 cm is good standing policy.

230750.explanation: This way we can help a large number of people secure housing all at once.

230753.explanation: An anti-vacancy ordinance prevents decay and makes more housing available.

230754.explanation: Westerkwartier has only recently joined the Municipal Credit Bank. First, it must be properly evaluated how well this meets the needs.

230755.explanation: D66 believes it is important that associations and volunteer work are well supported.

230756.explanation: D66 prioritizes better and safer bicycle connections. There will still be space for cars.

230759.explanation: Sounds like a sympathetic measure, but you can't use it to travel to a job interview in Groningen or Drachten because you are only allowed to travel within the municipality of Westerkwartier. Moreover, you cannot travel from Leek to Zuidhorn because that is only possible via Groningen.

230760.explanation: We need to invest now to achieve lower costs in the long term. We want to accomplish this by deploying more effective and faster treatments, but making the switch costs money.

230761.explanation: Families in vulnerable situations already receive support to get out of problems.

230762.explanation: People have the right to be in public spaces healthy and without smoke nuisance.

230763.explanation: Currently, there is in principle sufficient support, whether or not through tailored solutions.

230764.explanation: Supporting informal caregivers is important, but in times of budget cuts, an annual contribution is difficult to afford.

230707.explanation: The CDA is not in favor of a referendum. The municipal council is elected to make difficult trade-offs. It is important to involve citizens in other ways on important topics.

230709.explanation: The CDA believes that the burden on residents should remain as low as possible and that in the case of a possible increase, not only culture and sports but also other themes important to residents should be considered.

230711.explanation: The CDA does not want to further extend store opening hours on Sundays. The current Sunday opening hours are the result of a balanced compromise, which the CDA does not want to tamper with. The CDA especially wants to offer small entrepreneurs space for relaxation and reflection.

230712.explanation: The CDA supports energy cooperatives and neighborhood residents who take initiatives to make the environment more sustainable. The municipality can support this.

230713.explanation: The CDA favors a stricter asylum policy but believes the process should be faster. If residents or organizations, such as the Salvation Army, offer people help, the CDA believes that help should not be punishable.

230714.explanation: The CDA believes that the refugee is not the problem but the total housing supply. However, at the moment, local residents have too little chance of getting a home. Through efforts by the national CDA, the regulation can be changed so that young people can stay in their own village.

230715.explanation: The CDA wants waste to be processed neatly and responsibly. Unlimited free bulky waste can lead to more waste and higher costs for everyone. Therefore, we choose a fair arrangement that stimulates reuse and keeps the burden manageable.

230716.explanation: The CDA opposes reversing plans that have already been decided. This makes the government unreliable to residents and also leads to high planning damage. The CDA is not in favor of new large-scale solar parks on agricultural land.

230717.explanation: The CDA favors more 'private' cultural events in our municipality. This benefits the livability of the villages.

230718.explanation: The CDA supports healthy and safe schools. This promotes children's learning performance. The climate and condition of the schools are substandard in several places and must be addressed.

230719.explanation: The CDA wants to increase safety in our municipality. More municipal enforcement officers (BOAs) can contribute to this but do not solve the cause. The CDA wants to tackle the problem and is therefore looking for another solution than the deployment of police and/or BOAs.

230720.explanation: The CDA opts for peace and livability in our villages. Coffeeshops require a lot of enforcement and do not fit everywhere. Therefore, we see no reason to increase their number and prefer to focus on prevention, supervision, and combating illegal trade.

230721.explanation: The CDA wants to increase safety in our municipality. Camera surveillance is not a solution to the problem but causes the problem to shift. The CDA wants to tackle the problem and is therefore looking for another solution.

230722.explanation: The CDA believes that equipping BOAs with tasers does not contribute to a better position for the BOAs.

230723.explanation: The Housing Act mandates that 30% of new construction must be social housing. The CDA wants to comply with the laws. The CDA favors a good mix of affordable owner-occupied and rental homes in various segments.

230724.explanation: The CDA believes that owner-occupied homes are meant to live in, not to speculate with. A three-year occupancy obligation helps starters and prevents quick resale. There should still be room for reasonable exceptions, for example in cases of divorce, work, or care.

230725.explanation: The CDA believes that residents can take responsibility for their own homes. Fewer aesthetic regulations provide more freedom, less delay, and a government that trusts residents.

230726.explanation: The CDA finds the current income limits for minimum arrangements appropriate. However, the CDA thinks accessibility of the arrangements should be improved.

230727.explanation: The CDA wants to support residents with a low income but believes that everyone should contribute based on their ability to pay. Therefore, we choose targeted debt forgiveness, so people are helped without making the system unnecessarily complicated.

230728.explanation: The CDA believes that where parking nuisance exists, more parking spaces must be created. In new residential areas, the CDA wants to raise the standard to at least two cars per home.

230729.explanation: The CDA wants you to be able to drive safely at 50 km/h on the main roads within urban areas. The standard in residential streets is 30 km/h.

230730.explanation: Menstrual products are not a luxury. By making them free and accessible, we help people who need it and ensure no one has to be ashamed.

230731.explanation: The CDA believes it is important that residents participate in complex issues through clear participation. A formal citizens' council as an advisory body is not our preference.

230732.explanation: The CDA believes that young people must be able to participate in decisions that affect them. A youth council ensures that their ideas and experiences are incorporated into municipal policy.

230733.explanation: The CDA believes that the municipal counter should not be a static counter. The focus lies on (digital) proximity, realism, and affordability.

230734.explanation: The CDA believes a simple notification is sufficient, saving volunteers a lot of extra work.

230735.explanation: The CDA does not want to take on insurmountable financial risks but wants to look for other ways to help sports clubs in distress.

230736.explanation: The CDA believes culture in the broad sense is very important for society and that the current budget should be sufficient for this.

230737.explanation: The CDA supports more amenities in the villages. It is important to match supply and demand while also considering the total (annual) costs. At this moment, it is not realistic for Aduard to get a swimming pool.

230738.explanation: The CDA finds that the current regulatory burden regarding the environment is stifling, especially for small entrepreneurs, and that it can be greatly simplified.

230740.explanation: The CDA believes that burial must be affordable for everyone, so that residents can say goodbye to their loved ones in a dignified way without high costs forcing difficult choices.

230741.explanation: The CDA is willing to contribute to regulations in this area but it must not have negative financial consequences.

230742.explanation: The CDA supports the distribution law and favors shelter at multiple smaller locations in neighborhoods/villages. With this, the CDA wants to ensure better integration of asylum seekers in neighborhoods/villages and reduce pressure on one location or village.

230744.explanation: The CDA finds it important that there are sufficient charging points for electric cars. These should be placed based on demand, where people with electric cars live and work. Residents must be able to apply for this at the municipality. A minimum of 1,000 is a good target.

230745.explanation: The CDA believes that this choice should lie with the hospitality entrepreneur themselves.

230749.explanation: We believe that residents are responsible for their own property. Only truly valuable, monumental, and characteristic trees will be protected, with the aim of having and maintaining a green and livable municipality.

230750.explanation: The CDA believes this can give a major boost to solving the housing shortage in our municipality. Local residents must be properly involved in the implementation.

230753.explanation: The CDA wants to tackle vacancy but believes a generic tax is not the right approach. Vacancy often has legitimate causes. We prefer tailored solutions, redevelopment, and active utilization of properties.

230754.explanation: The CDA believes that a municipal credit bank managed in-house gives the municipality control and oversight of administration, helps prevent debts, and ensures careful and effective use of resources.

230755.explanation: The CDA believes volunteers provide indispensable work for many social organizations and that a free certificate of good conduct (VOG) makes it easier for them to be active, while preventing these organizations from incurring unnecessary costs.

230756.explanation: The Westerkwartier municipality has a lot of rural area with large distances. The CDA believes that cars are indispensable for accessibility in our municipality.

230759.explanation: The CDA stands with residents who have financial difficulties. However, free public transport for everyone with a low income is not a targeted solution. We choose tailored help that truly supports people.

230760.explanation: The CDA believes costs must be lowered but never at the expense of our children. Smarter organization and working on prevention is feasible, but shutting off care funding solves nothing. The CDA has long advocated for care management centered on the child, not the provider's wallet.

230761.explanation: The CDA believes that couples therapy is a personal responsibility and that the costs should not be borne by the municipality.

230762.explanation: The CDA primarily stands for the good health of our residents. This contributes to that.

230763.explanation: The CDA does not believe any one group should receive priority for rental housing. However, the CDA believes priority rules for allocating rental homes should be better balanced so that other vulnerable groups (e.g., separated parents) also have opportunities for housing allocation.

230764.explanation: The CDA believes the caregiver allowance, currently a VVV voucher of 50 euros, may be increased to at least €250 in cash. Caregivers do important work and often sacrifice themselves. With this amount, they can, for example, treat themselves to a day out.

230707.explanation: A referendum undermines the current democratic system based on electing municipal council members who receive a mandate from the voters to make decisions over a four-year term. Residents can also influence matters through, for example, the right to speak.

230709.explanation: The VVD does not want new increases in property tax (OZB). We reject attempts by other parties to increase tax burdens on property ownership. Housing costs for residents and businesses in Westerkwartier are already too high. The VVD chooses affordability. Your property is not a "cash cow" for the municipality.

230711.explanation: Entrepreneurs themselves are best suited to decide when their business is open or closed. It is not up to the municipality to determine when a business opens or closes or when residents want to shop. This applies not only to Sundays but also to public holidays.

230712.explanation: Generating sustainable energy is the responsibility of residents, entrepreneurs, and social organizations. We do believe the municipality should provide space for this, for example by imposing fewer rules and less interference.

230713.explanation: These people are confirmed not to be refugees and must safely return to their country of origin. No aid or shelter is offered within Westerkwartier. The mayor collaborates with, among others, the Public Prosecution Service and the national government to ensure a timely return to the home country.

230714.explanation: Residents of Westerkwartier, who sometimes have waited for years for suitable housing, have just as much right to affordable housing. We find the priority position of status holders unjust and inexplicable compared to all other housing seekers.

230715.explanation: The principle is that the polluter pays. After all, it costs money to process waste. Disposing of bulky waste cannot be free, but we strive for the lowest possible price. This also aims to prevent littering and illegal dumping.

230716.explanation: The VVD does not want solar parks in Westerkwartier on valuable agricultural land in rural areas. We choose solar panels on roofs and parking lots. It should also be possible for (agricultural) entrepreneurs to install solar panels on roofs.

230717.explanation: We choose an enterprising and thriving cultural sector, which primarily bears responsibility for attracting visitors itself. The VVD wants to help the cultural and event sector by reducing regulatory burdens – for example, by abolishing the permit requirement for small events.

230718.explanation: We would like school building construction to proceed in a planned manner – translated into a multi-year investment program. This provides clarity to schools and residents, who sometimes have waited years for certainty. This allows educational housing to continue while keeping finances under control.

230719.explanation: Enforcement starts with sufficient supervision, and enough enforcement officers are needed for this. They increase the visibility of enforcement and can act against nuisance and (minor) crime – also in cooperation with the police. We want to tackle nuisance, violence, and crime rigorously.

230720.explanation: Westerkwartier is a rural municipality. As far as we are concerned, a coffeeshop does not fit in our village centers – often resulting in nuisance and insecurity. People can, for example, go to Drachten or Groningen to visit a coffeeshop.

230721.explanation: We believe the mayor should be allowed to make actual use of targeted camera surveillance where necessary. The safety of our residents, entrepreneurs, and visitors is paramount. Targeted camera surveillance can help maintain public order and safety by increasing the chance of apprehension.

230722.explanation: We are in favor of good equipment for our enforcement officers. From good employer practice, the equipment of enforcement officers should be expanded so that they have the right modern tools and training to stay safe themselves and increase the chance of apprehension.

230723.explanation: The inability to realize enough social rental housing should never hinder building houses. The VVD wants the municipality not to be fixated on achieving a certain percentage. Including 30% is a new rule that could delay housing construction.

230724.explanation: The VVD is the party of owning your own home. Those who buy a house in our municipality have paid with money they worked hard to earn. It is your house. It is not up to the municipality to decide what you do with your house.

230725.explanation: No one wants to live in an ugly house. We too often see that the aesthetic regulations cause unnecessary delays in procedures. The aesthetic committee also causes unnecessary rules and annoying interference. Residents and entrepreneurs should have more space by abolishing aesthetic requirements.

230726.explanation: There should be a social safety net for residents who (temporarily or permanently) cannot work – not a hammock. We offer intensive tailored guidance towards work. Residents' personal responsibility remains paramount. The principle is that everyone contributes and makes an effort.

230727.explanation: There should be a social safety net for residents who (temporarily or permanently) cannot work – not a hammock. We offer intensive tailored guidance towards work. Residents' personal responsibility remains paramount. The principle is that everyone contributes and makes an effort.

230728.explanation: Parking standards should never cause residents to be unable to park their car near their home. Residents must also be able to receive visitors decently. Westerkwartier is a rural municipality: our residents and entrepreneurs simply need their cars.

230729.explanation: Speeding often occurs in villages and residential areas. Responsibility for traffic safety primarily lies with road users who drive too fast or behave inconsiderately. We do not solve this with a speed reduction to 30 km/h.

230730.explanation: Providing free tampons and sanitary pads is not a municipal task. We understand that monthly purchase of tampons and pads can be a cost. Social initiatives are welcome within our municipality.

230731.explanation: A citizens' assembly undermines the current democratic system based on electing municipal council members who receive a mandate from voters to make decisions over a four-year period. Residents can also influence matters through, for example, the right to speak.

230732.explanation: Too often people think from the instrument (“establishing a youth council”) rather than the goal (“engagement of youth in local politics”). We support guest lessons at schools and the course “Active in Politics.” Young people can also become members of the VVD.

230733.explanation: Multiple municipal counters are not practical and financially unwise. We prefer good service at the desk, by phone, and digitally. In our view, civil servants can also come on location when needed, so a visit to the town hall is no longer necessary.

230734.explanation: The VVD wants to reduce rules and bureaucracy without compromising safety. We strive to abolish as many permits as possible and replace them with general rules or notification requirements. No longer filling out 60 pages of forms for a fun initiative.

230735.explanation: It is the municipality's task to create the conditions for a healthy club life. The VVD sees advantages in privatization to increase the independence of sports clubs. This can be done through collaborations between clubs or, if necessary, mergers.

230736.explanation: The principle is that the cultural offering in Westerkwartier should come from society itself. We want to make the cultural sector less dependent on municipal subsidies. It is up to the municipality and politicians to manage tax money efficiently and frugally.

230737.explanation: If residents, entrepreneurs, and/or social organizations can independently manage to build a swimming pool in Aduard without using tax money, the VVD considers it a great plan to realize a swimming pool in Aduard.

230738.explanation: The municipality must not impose more or stricter (environmental) regulations than legally required. The municipality can contribute to the local economy by giving entrepreneurs space, reducing bureaucracy and regulatory pressure, so that entrepreneurs have room to do business.

230740.explanation: For the VVD, it is important that the costs of burial are paid by the user and that a cost-covering fee is charged.

230741.explanation: Actively approaching new businesses strengthens the local economy in Westerkwartier. This creates new jobs and strengthens the economic climate for existing local companies. We see opportunities for the area around the A7 for the establishment of startups.

230742.explanation: We believe that the reception of asylum seekers should take place in the region of their country of origin. If legislation forces us to accommodate asylum seekers, we want to take into account, among other things, support base, duration, capacity, and local circumstances.

230744.explanation: We choose realistic policies and do not want to lock ourselves into (possibly) unattainable objectives. We should not be blinded by a paper reality but focus on good plans. Installing charging points partly falls within the personal responsibility of residents and entrepreneurs.

230745.explanation: Hospitality policy should strike a good balance between space for entrepreneurs and protection of the immediate surroundings and public order. When this balance is good, hospitality entrepreneurs can, in our opinion, also count on more space for terraces and extended opening hours (also after midnight).

230749.explanation: People can very well decide for themselves whether a tree can be cut down on their own property. However, we could imagine that a list of protected trees and wooded banks, for example in the coulisse landscape, could be drawn up to preserve these in the landscape.

230750.explanation: Large-scale housing plans - for example in the form of a new residential area, forms of high-rise buildings, or even building a new village center - are up for discussion because the need for housing is very high. Feasibility and affordability will depend, among other things, on the content of the housing plans.

230753.explanation: We are not in favor of introducing new municipal taxes, as that only leads to less disposable income for residents and entrepreneurs. Furthermore, it is not up to the municipality to decide what you must do with your property. After all, it is your ownership.

230754.explanation: Currently, there is an investigation into the advantages and disadvantages of a municipal credit bank. The VVD currently has no principled reasons to be for or against this statement.

230755.explanation: It is not up to the municipality to issue a Certificate of Conduct (VOG) for free. Costs are incurred once and these must be paid. Organizations can, if necessary, partially pay the application for a VOG for volunteers.

230756.explanation: One of the government’s core tasks is to ensure good infrastructure connections so that people can easily and safely travel from A to B. Westerkwartier is a rural municipality. Our residents simply need cars.

230759.explanation: There should be a social safety net for residents who (temporarily or permanently) cannot work – not a hammock. We offer intensive tailored guidance towards work. Residents' personal responsibility remains paramount. The principle is that everyone contributes and makes an effort.

230760.explanation: The rising costs of youth care require targeted action by setting clear limits on youth assistance, effective prevention policies, recognizing and addressing signals as early as possible, deploying lighter support instead of heavier support where possible, and so on.

230761.explanation: In our opinion, it is not a municipal task to reimburse couples therapy with tax money. We see this as a classic example of personal responsibility.

230762.explanation: In general, we do not like municipal meddling and regulatory pressure - also not in the case of an (overall) smoking ban. We really see this as a personal responsibility and a topic of conversation within society among residents, entrepreneurs, and social organizations.

230763.explanation: The municipality has a duty to provide social shelter. As a social safety net - not a hammock. This does not mean that homeless people should have automatic priority when allocating social rental housing. The personal responsibility of residents remains paramount.

230764.explanation: Initially, residents themselves are looked at to arrange support as much as possible with the help of their own environment. A social safety net usually aligns better with people's own wishes. It is not up to the municipality to grant a monetary amount to informal caregivers.

230709.explanation: Tax increases are not what the SGP wants, especially not for a specific purpose.

230711.explanation: The SGP wants to promote Sunday rest for the well-being of residents and protect small entrepreneurs from excessive workload. It goes against the social welfare of the resident.

230712.explanation: The municipality publicizes green schemes and energy coaches and supports energy cooperatives of residents. This does not necessarily mean that the municipality provides financial support for sustainable energy generation; there are regional and national schemes that provide for this.

230713.explanation: The SGP advocates hospitality for refugees, but the reception of asylum seekers who are illegal does not fit this.

230714.explanation: The reception of status holders must be balanced with the legitimate interests of the local population, including sufficient housing. Provision is made for places for status holders, and a reasonable appeal is made to society.

230715.explanation: Well-separated waste is easier to recycle. Simply disposing of bulky waste without proper sorting is not what the SGP wants. Separated and well-recyclable waste could be brought in for free. Those are the new raw materials.

230716.explanation: Solar energy on roofs has priority over solar fields. For solar fields, there are frameworks: temporality and double land use. No solar fields will be placed on locations suitable for food production.

230717.explanation: The municipality encourages initiatives and events that connect society and respect our basic Christian norms and values.

230718.explanation: Ventilation and indoor climate deserve structural attention, and sufficient fresh air is essential for health and an optimal learning environment. Where necessary, old buildings will be screened and addressed accordingly.

230719.explanation: BOAs (public order officers) have a lot of local knowledge. More focus should be placed on this in combating undermining. It is necessary that BOAs are better equipped and supported to effectively address misconduct in the neighborhood.

230720.explanation: Addiction through recreational substances is not good for society. The chance of crime or nuisance is high, and the well-being of residents is not served by the establishment of a coffeeshop.

230721.explanation: No Big Brother. Deploying BOAs and police is a more appropriate measure to counteract nuisance.

230722.explanation: If BOAs are given heavier responsibilities in exceptional cases, their equipment must be adjusted accordingly so they can work safely.

230723.explanation: The municipality, together with housing corporations and project developers, commits to an appropriate housing supply. The share of social rental, affordable ownership, and mid-range rental is aligned with local needs, not national quotas.

230724.explanation: Unreasonable demand because the resident does not know how their living and working situation will be in 3 years.

230725.explanation: Building regulations can be relaxed and largely abolished. Complete abolition is too drastic and may lead to cultural aspects being lost.

230726.explanation: The municipality promotes the work of social organizations active in poverty reduction, guidance of status holders, debt counseling, and combating loneliness.

230727.explanation: Exemption from municipal taxes for residents with a low income.

230728.explanation: The increasing number of cars calls for smart and future-proof solutions. In addition to good public transport facilities, the SGP remains in favor of using one’s own means of transport.

230729.explanation: Within residential neighborhoods yes, but not on through roads. It is not appropriate or necessary to introduce a 30 km/h speed limit everywhere.

230730.explanation: Providing everything for free is not the intention. Where is the limit? Will we also provide bandages for free? It is better to have a solid general poverty policy than to provide certain products for free.

230731.explanation: The municipal council is the people's representative body. Furthermore, citizens are free to organize themselves, for example in village interest organizations. A citizens' assembly adds nothing essential to this.

230732.explanation: It is possible for young people to run for the municipal council, as the youngest lead candidate, Tim Venekamp, demonstrates. Moreover, young people, like all residents, can always advise their representatives and, for example, speak at council meetings.

230733.explanation: The distance to the municipality is large for many people, often even literally. Investment is being made in the municipality's presence in all former municipalities, at least one day per week.

230734.explanation: There should be no obstacles to a neighborhood barbecue, because togetherness is paramount. For events up to 100 people, a notification is sufficient according to the SGP. Only, the noise must remain within generally accepted limits.

230735.explanation: If the association otherwise can no longer make ends meet, that is an option.

230736.explanation: Cultural activities must originate from private initiative.

230737.explanation: There are swimming facilities in Zuidhorn and nearby in the city.

230738.explanation: Entrepreneurs and farmers deserve space. Regulations and taxes should be limited to what is reasonable. There is room for an economy that fits the identity of the municipality.

230740.explanation: Rates for burial rights need not be cost-covering, so that everyone can purchase burial rights.

230741.explanation: Entrepreneurs should be given space, but creating separate buildings for this is unnecessary.

230742.explanation: The SGP advocates hospitality for refugees, but the reception of asylum seekers must fit the nature and scale of the community.

230744.explanation: There will be sufficient charging stations for electric cars and bicycles in public spaces.

230745.explanation: It contributes to health, livability, and residential pleasure when hospitality establishments close on time. Closing before midnight is the goal.

230750.explanation: The SGP prefers infill development over expansion to handle available space carefully. At the same time, it is clear that expansion is often necessary to solve the housing shortage. In case of expansion, consideration is given to the landscape, preservation of greenery, and the cultural-historical layout of the area. This requires plans that carefully balance growth and preservation of the area's identity.

230753.explanation: As a government, we cannot determine what someone does with their property.

230754.explanation: The current solutions via the Municipal Credit Bank Drenthe (GKB) are fine.

230755.explanation: A large part of our municipality runs on the quiet strength of volunteers; we should not financially burden them.

230756.explanation: In addition to good public transport facilities, the SGP also supports the use of private means of transport.

230760.explanation: The SGP believes that cuts in youth care are irresponsible if prevention is not prioritized. By critically reviewing the costs incurred, the SGP does not want to cut but to reassess whether the approach is sound and thereby reduce costs.

230761.explanation: The SGP opts to support stable home situations with low-threshold access to relationship therapy.

230762.explanation: The SGP supports a healthy lifestyle but opposes regulatory pressure.

230763.explanation: The risk of attracting homeless people from the city of Groningen is too great with this. Involving organizations such as the Salvation Army or churches is preferred.

230764.explanation: Informal caregivers deserve a generous token of appreciation every year.

statements.title: Dropping off bulky waste at the recycling center should be free.

titleParts.glossary: Waste that is larger than an ordinary trash bin, such as old furniture.
A place where citizens and businesses can bring different kinds of waste.

statements.title: The residents of Westerkwartier should be able to express their opinion on important issues through a referendum.

titleParts.text: De inwoners van Westerkwartier moeten over belangrijke onderwerpen hun mening kunnen geven via een
